---
title: "4.10 Rotate, Reverse, and Parallel Lists"
subtitle: "Implementing ArrayList Algorithms"
format:
  clean-revealjs:
    slide-number: true
    css: unit4-shared.css
lang: zh-CN
---

## Course Directory

### Return to the course outline

[← Back to AP CSA / 返回课程目录](../../index.html)

## Shift/Rotate an `ArrayList`

### Rotate elements left or right

We can write code that shifts or rotates elements left or right in an `ArrayList`.

The source example rotates the elements in an `ArrayList` to the right by one position.

## Mixed-Up Code

### `parsonsprob:: rotateArrayListParsons`

The following method should rotate the elements in an `ArrayList` of `Integer` objects to the right by one position.

The blocks have been mixed up and include extra blocks that are not needed.

::: {.code-scroll .compact style="max-height: 310px;"}
```java
public static void rotateRight(ArrayList<Integer> nums)
{
=====
    int last = nums.get(nums.size() - 1);
=====
    int last = nums[nums.length - 1]; // extra block
=====
    for (int i = nums.size() - 1; i > 0; i--)
    {
=====
    for (int i = nums.size() - 1; i >= 0; i--)
    { // extra block
=====
        nums.set(i, nums.get(i - 1));
=====
        nums[i] = nums[i - 1]; // extra block
=====
    } // end for loop
=====
    nums.set(0, last);
=====
    nums[0] = last; // extra block
=====
} // end rotateRight method
```
:::

## Mixed-Up Code: Correct Order

### rotateArrayListParsons

```java
public static void rotateRight(ArrayList<Integer> nums)
{
    int last = nums.get(nums.size() - 1);
    for (int i = nums.size() - 1; i > 0; i--)
    {
        nums.set(i, nums.get(i - 1));
    } // end for loop
    nums.set(0, last);
} // end rotateRight method
```

## Coding Exercise

### `activecode:: rotateNewList`

Write a method `rotateLeft` that rotates the elements in an `ArrayList` of `Integer` objects to the left by one position.

## TestRotate Starter

::: {.code-scroll}
```java
import java.util.*;

public class TestRotate
{
    public static void rotateLeft(ArrayList<Integer> nums)
    {
        // Add code here
    }

    public static void main(String[] args)
    {
        // instantiate ArrayList and fill with Integers
        ArrayList<Integer> values = new ArrayList<Integer>();
        int[] arrnums = {1, 5, 7};
        for (int i = 0; i < arrnums.length; i++)
        {
            values.add(arrnums[i]);
        }
        rotateLeft(values);
        System.out.println("Expected Result: [5, 7, 1]");
        System.out.println("Your Result: " + values);
    }
}
```
:::

## TestRotate Test Targets

### Output and method use

The output from `main` should be:

```text
Expected Result: [5, 7, 1]
Your Result: [5, 7, 1]
```

The Runestone tests also check that the code contains the `set` method.

## TestRotate Extra Call

### rotateLeft with a longer list

Test 2 calls `TestRotate.rotateLeft(list)` with:

```text
[2, 4, 0, -2]
```

Expected list after the method call:

```text
[4, 0, -2, 2]
```

## Reversing an `ArrayList`

### Add to the beginning of a new list

The following examples reverse the order of the elements in an `ArrayList` by adding each element to the beginning of a new `String` or `ArrayList`.

## Mixed-Up Code

### `parsonsprob:: reverseArrayListParsons`

The following method should traverse through an `ArrayList` of `String` objects and print out the elements in reverse order.

For `{"cat", "dog", "mouse"}`, it should print:

```text
mouse, dog, cat,
```

## Mixed-Up Blocks

### reverseArrayListParsons

```java
public static void printBackwards(ArrayList<String> myList)
{
=====
  for (int i = myList.size() - 1; i >= 0; i--)
  {
=====
  for (int i = myList.size() - 1; i > 0; i--)
  { // extra block
=====
     System.out.print(myList.get(i) + ", ");
=====
     System.out.print(myList[i] + ", "); // extra block
=====
  } // end for loop
} // end printBackwards method
```

## Mixed-Up Code: Correct Order

### reverseArrayListParsons

```java
public static void printBackwards(ArrayList<String> myList)
{
  for (int i = myList.size() - 1; i >= 0; i--)
  {
     System.out.print(myList.get(i) + ", ");
  } // end for loop
} // end printBackwards method
```

## Coding Exercise

### `activecode:: reverseNewList`

Complete the method `reverse` below to return an `ArrayList` containing `Integer` objects in the reversed order of the `ArrayList` parameter `list`.

Hint: use a `for` loop with one line inside it to add to the new list.

## TestReverse Starter

::: {.code-scroll}
```java
import java.util.*;

public class TestReverse
{
    public static ArrayList<Integer> reverse(ArrayList<Integer> list)
    {
        ArrayList<Integer> reversed = new ArrayList<Integer>();
        // Use an enhanced for loop here

        return reversed;
    }

    public static void main(String[] args)
    {
        // instantiate ArrayList and fill with Integers
        ArrayList<Integer> values = new ArrayList<Integer>();
        for (int i = 0; i < 4; i++)
        {
            values.add(i);
        }
        ArrayList<Integer> result = reverse(values);
        System.out.println("Expected Result:\t [3, 2, 1, 0]");
        System.out.println("Your Result:\t\t " + result);
    }
}
```
:::

## TestReverse Test Targets

### Output and insertion position

The output from `main` should be:

```text
Expected Result: [3, 2, 1, 0]
Your Result:     [3, 2, 1, 0]
```

The Runestone tests also check for a call that adds to the beginning of `reversed`.

Target code fragment:

```java
reversed.add(0
```

## TestReverse Extra Call

### reverse with `[2, 4, 6]`

Test 2 calls `TestReverse.reverse(list)` with:

```text
[2, 4, 6]
```

Expected return value:

```text
[6, 4, 2]
```

## Multiple or Parallel Data Structures

### Traverse structures simultaneously

Some algorithms require multiple `String`, array, or `ArrayList` objects to be traversed simultaneously.

For example, the following code traverses two parallel `ArrayList` objects that hold the grades for different tests.

## Coding Exercise

### `activecode:: parallelArrayLists`

Complete the code below to add the items in parallel `ArrayList` objects to `total`.

## ParallelTests Starter

::: {.code-scroll}
```java
import java.util.*;

public class ParallelTests
{
    public static void main(String[] args)
    {
        ArrayList<Integer> test1Grades = new ArrayList<Integer>();
        ArrayList<Integer> test2Grades = new ArrayList<Integer>();
        test1Grades.add(100);
        test2Grades.add(100);
        test1Grades.add(80);
        test2Grades.add(70);
        test1Grades.add(70);
        test2Grades.add(90);
        double total = 0;
        // Write 1 indexed for loop that adds
        // test1Grades and test2Grades at index i to total


        int numberOfGrades = test1Grades.size() * 2;
        System.out.println("Average over two tests: " + total / numberOfGrades);
    }
}
```
:::

## ParallelTests Test Targets

### Output and loop count

The output from `main` should be:

```text
Average over two tests: 85.0
```

The Runestone tests also count the number of `for` loops.

Expected loop count:

```text
1 loop
```

## Classroom Check

### A complete answer should include

- store the value that would be overwritten before shifting or rotating
- use `set(index, value)` when changing an `ArrayList` item
- use `add(0, value)` to build a reversed list by prepending
- traverse parallel structures with the same index variable
- preserve starter comments and test expectations from each source exercise

## End

### 4.10 Rotate, Reverse, and Parallel Lists

Next: 2D Array Creation and Access.
